coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots wrapped square-plate-like MnO2 nanocomposite as a fluorescent turn-on sensor for glutathione
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qi
Li, Lingfang
Wang, Xiaodong
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: Benefiting from their excellent optical absorption and electron transfer properties, manganese dioxide nanomaterials have been widely applied for fluorescence-based nanosensors. In this work, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) wrapped square-plate-like MnO2 nanocomposite was synthesized from potassium permanganate via an in situ redox procedure under ultrasonication with poly(allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH) being involved. Through an effective fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process and inner filter effect (IFE) between GQDs and MnO2, the fluorescence of GQDs was quenched. Furthermore, the introduction of glutathione (GSH) decomposed MnO2 and caused fluorescence recovery of GQDs. Therefore, a MnO2 mediated nanosensor was established for fluorescent turn-on sensing of GSH. A satisfactory linear range was found to be 0.07–70 μM and the detection limit was as low as 48 nM. Besides, the fluorescent recognition of cancer cells using GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite was achieved because of the obviously higher GSH content in cancer microenvironment than normal cells. This nanosensor was constructed directly in GQDs solution in the presence of PAH without the complicated modifications or connections, making it a facile and novel nanosensor for GSH.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MnO2
Graphene quantum dots
Glutathione
Fluorescent turn-on

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQDs and GQDs@MnO2 nanocomposite
Characterization of GQDs and GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite
Fluorescence response of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite to GSH
GSH determination in sample and cancer cell discrimination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images of (A) GQDs and (B) GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite. Insets were corresponding HRTEM images. The (C) SEM and (D) re-coloured SEM images of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite.
The XPS (A) Mn 2p, (B) O 1s (C) C1s and (D) N 1s spectra of as-prepared GQDs@MnO2 nanocomposite.

(A) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of as-prepared GQDs and GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite. Insets: the photos of (1) GQDs and (2) GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite. (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of the system. Inset: the photos of (3) GQDs, (4) GQDs-MnO2 and (5) GQDs-MnO2+GSH under UV light. (C) The comparison of UV absorption of MnO2 and fluorescent emission of GQDs. (D) Time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra from GQDs only, GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite and GQDs-MnO2+GSH.

The time-dependent (A) absorption and (B) fluorescence intensity of GQDs-MnO2 in the presence of GSH (50 μM). The pH-dependent (C) fluorescence intensity and (D) fluorescence recovery efficiency of GQDs-MnO2 in the presence of GSH (50 μM).
(A) The linear calibration for GSH determination using GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite. (B) The selectivity of GSH determination against possible interferences.

(A) The absorption intensity and (B) fluorescence intensity of GQDs-MnO2-GSH at different intervals under different pH values. The CLSM fluorescence images of (C) L02 and (D) SMMC-7721 cells after incubation with GQDs-MnO2.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite and GSH stimulated fluorescence turn-off-on process.
Determination of GSH in human serum.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots wrapped square-plate-like MnO2 nanocomposite as a fluorescent turn-on sensor for glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, PR China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Department, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan, 030008, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Department, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan, 030008, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Benefiting from their excellent optical absorption and electron transfer properties, manganese dioxide nanomaterials have been widely applied for fluorescence-based nanosensors. In this work, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) wrapped square-plate-like MnO2 nanocomposite was synthesized from potassium permanganate via an in situ redox procedure under ultrasonication with poly(allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH) being involved. Through an effective fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process and inner filter effect (IFE) between GQDs and MnO2, the fluorescence of GQDs was quenched. Furthermore, the introduction of glutathione (GSH) decomposed MnO2 and caused fluorescence recovery of GQDs. Therefore, a MnO2 mediated nanosensor was established for fluorescent turn-on sensing of GSH. A satisfactory linear range was found to be 0.07–70 μM and the detection limit was as low as 48 nM. Besides, the fluorescent recognition of cancer cells using GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite was achieved because of the obviously higher GSH content in cancer microenvironment than normal cells. This nanosensor was constructed directly in GQDs solution in the presence of PAH without the complicated modifications or connections, making it a facile and novel nanosensor for GSH.

Schematic illustration of the synthesis of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite and GSH stimulated fluorescence turn-off-on process.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Biological thiols are indispensable bio-molecules playing crucial roles in maintaining biological redox homeostasis. As the most abundant (1–10 mM) cellular nonprotein thiol, glutathione (GSH) is widely recognized as the best defence against the potential toxicity of reactive oxygen species and plays key parts in pathological conditions [1–3]. It has been proved that many diseases including acquired immune deficiency syndrome, human immunodeficiency virus, Alzheimer's disease, heart problems, diabetes, etc. are generally related to the improper content of intracellular GSH [4–6]. More significantly, an obvious discrepancy of GSH content is reported in tumour cells against healthy ones [7,8], and thus the overexpressed GSH is utilized to indicate cancer for diagnostic purposes [9–11]. Therefore, it makes significant sense to monitor the concentration of GSH in biological systems.

Manganese dioxide nanomaterials occupy proper posts because of their advantageous properties like low cost, high surface area, large molar extinction coefficients, good biocompatibility [12–14]. Particularly, MnO2 nanomaterials can be decomposed into nontoxic Mn2+ in the human body and subsequently excreted by the kidney, which proves that MnO2 poses no long-term toxic threat [15,16]. Thus, MnO2 nanomaterials, combining with their excellent optical absorption and electron transfer properties [17], are extensively applied for fluorescent nanosensors. Therefore, fluorescent materials including carbon dots [18–20], Au nanoclusters [21], Cu nanoclusters [22], metal complex [23], boron nitride quantum dots [6], silica nanospheres [24], and up-conversion nanoparticle [25] have been researched and developed to integrate with MnO2 nanomaterials for construction of fluorescent turn-off-on nanosensors, in which MnO2 nanomaterials acted as fluorescence quenching agents.

Specific to GSH sensing, MnO2 nanomaterials not only induce the fluorescence quenching but also provide the function of GSH identification through a unique redox reaction. Since the first report of MnO2 nanosheet-upconversion nanomaterials systems for detection of intracellular GSH [26], various MnO2 nanomaterials have been exploited to construct GSH stimulated fluorescent turn-off-on sensors in vivo or in vitro. To generate effective fluorescence quenching, the distance between manganese dioxide and fluorescent material needs to be kept within a short-range. Therefore, electrostatic interaction and chemical bonding are concerned for construct MnO2-based nanosensors. Carbon dots were designed and synthesized to combine with MnO2 nanomaterials through electrostatic absorption [27] and amidation reaction connection [28] for GSH detection, respectively. Fluorescent AgNPs were also connected with CPs@MnO2 nanocomposite by electrostatic attraction for GSH sensing [29]. In addition, direct hybrid of nano-MnO2 with fluorescent materials was also carried out, in which MnO2 was anchored on the surface of fluorescent materials to form nanocomposites. Potassium permanganate was introduced into carbon dots [30], g-C3N4 nanosheet [31] and metal-organic frameworks [32] to build GSH-responsive nanocomposites and applied into GSH sensing. However, necessary surface modifications of materials are needed in the electrostatic absorption and chemical bonding methods, so that in situ synthesis method have drawn much attention. But synthesis of MnO2 usually involved strict conditions such as high temperature, high pressure or long reaction time during in situ reaction, and most of these materials were nanosheets [33].

In this work, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) wrapped MnO2 nanocomposites were synthesized directly in GQDs solution from KMnO4 with the help of PAH. The special lamellar structures of GQDs and electrostatic aggregation induced by PAH resulted in the stack of materials and finally formed the unique square-plate-like MnO2 nanocomposites. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs quenched in the presence of MnO2 by means of fluorescence resonance energy transfer and recovered under GSH stimulating owing to the decomposition of the quencher. Thus, a GSH responsive fluorescent turn-off-on sensor was set up for in vitro and in vivo sensing. MnO2 nanomaterials in our work were obtained through a facile in situ redox reaction directly in GQDs solutions with the help of PAH. The reaction was performed within 15 min under open system, avoiding complicated equipment or reaction conditions. What's more, MnO2 nanomaterials were directly formed in GQDs to construct a square-plate like nanocomposite. Therefore, the facile in situ synthesis and brand-new morphology of MnO2-based nanocomposite make the present work advantageous and novel. The direct synthesis of nanosensor avoided the complicated modifications or connections, facilitating the MnO2-based sensors.

Glutathione (98%), poly(allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH, 95%) and citric acid (GR, 99.8%) were acquired from Aladdin Co. (Shanghai, China). Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) with analytical grade was obtained from Kemiou Co. (Tianjin, China). HAc-NaAc buffers with concentration of 0.02 M were prepared by titrating sodium acetate solutions using equal concentrations of NaOH or HAc solutions to the required pH values. Cells used in this work were from Shanxi Medical University (Taiyuan, China) and human serum sample was collected from a healthy volunteer at Shanxi Medical University.

The morphologies of GQDs and GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite were examined on a G2 F20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, America). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite was carried out on a JSM-7200F transmission electron microscope (JOEL, Japan). A Malvern Nano-ZS90 sizer was carried out for the size distribution test of products through dynamic light scattering (DLS). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS ULTRA DLD, Kratos) was applied for chemical composition investigation of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite. The UV–vis spectra of products were measured on a UV–Vis spectrometer (TU-1901, Puxi, China). The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan) and fluorescence lifetimes were tested on a lifetime and steady state spectrometer (FLS 1000, Edinburgh, UK). A confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Zeiss LSM-880, Germany) was applied for cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs and GQDs@MnO2 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots were synthesized with reference to a previous work with some modifications [34]. In a typical procedure, a 50 mL beaker containing 2 g citric acid powders was placed in a heating mantle. After heating at 200 °C for 30 min, liquids with orange colour were obtained, which indicated the formation of GQDs. Then 100 mL NaOH solutions with concentration of 10 mg mL−1 were introduced to dissolve the product. At last, the solutions were dialyzed to deionized water for 48 h and the GQDs powders were obtained through freeze-drying.

The GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite was synthesized by reduction of KMnO4 under ultrasonication. Typically, 500 μL PAH (40 mg mL−1) were added dropwise into 0.5 mL as-prepared GQDs solutions in an ultrasonic cleaner. The mixture became cloudy once PAH were added. Then 1 mL KMnO4 solution with concentration of 3.5 mg mL−1 were added and reacted for 15 min with ultrasonic disposal applied during the whole procedure. The purple mixture returned to clean and turned brown gradually, which demonstrated the formation of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite. The obtained solutions were dialyzed to deionized water for 2 days and kept at 4 °C.

At room temperature, 30 μL GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite solution were added into 870 μL HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 3), then 100 μL GSH solutions with different concentrations or buffer (blank for control) were introduced to the GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite solution. After reacting for 30 min, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. The fluorescence measurement parameters (excitation wavelength: 360 nm; excitation and emission slit widths: 5 nm) were set up for all the fluorescence measurement. For real sample determination, human serum was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm and diluted 300 times firstly. 30 μL GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite solution were added into 900 μL buffer before adding 100 μL human serum supernatant. Then GSH with standard concentrations (3, 25, 45 μM) were spiked. The fluorescence intensities of the mixtures were measured after reaction for 30 min. At last, the content of GSH in human serum sample and recovery of this method were calculated.

The cytotoxicity of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite was measured via 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) method based on SMMC-7721 cells. After incubation for 24 h under optimal conditions, GQDs-MnO2 NPs nanocomposite solutions with different concentrations (0, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 μg/mL) were added into cell culture solution for another 24 h. Then, MTT solution with concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1 were added and the mixture were reacted for 4 h. At last, an enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) was carried out to record the absorption intensity at 490 nm.

Cell imaging was performed to investigate the capacity of GQDs-MnO2 NPs nanocomposite to sense GSH and recognize cancer cells. Cancer cells (SMMC-7721) and corresponding normal cells (L02) were firstly grown into dishes to cultivation. Then, the cells were washed by PBS solutions (0.01 M, pH = 7.4) for three times and 50 μL GQDs-MnO2 NPs nanocomposite were introduced before 3 h incubation. At last, the cell lines were rinsed with PBS solutions for another three times before imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs and GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots were synthesized through the pyrolysis of citric acid. Fig. 1 A displayed the TEM image of GQDs and the HRTEM pattern showed obvious lattice fringes of 0.24 nm, which corresponded to the lattice plane of graphene (110) [35]. Fig. S1 exhibited the size distribution of as-prepared GQDs and an average diameter of 4.5 nm was obtained. Further, GQDs wrapped MnO2 nanocomposite was prepared through an in situ redox reaction between KMnO4 and GQDs under ultrasonication with PAH being involved. Firstly, cationic polyelectrolyte of PAH was connected onto the negative-charged GQDs via electrostatic interaction inducing the aggregation of GQDs. Then KMnO4 as a precursor was added, enabling the redox between PAH and KMnO4. At last, MnO2 was formed and grew into a special shape to hybridize GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite. Fig. S2 showed the photographs during this synthesis process. Once addition of PAH, the pale-yellow solution of GQDs (Fig. S2A) turned into cloudy immediately indicating the formation of GQDs-PAH (Fig. S2B). After reaction with KMnO4, the mixture became clear and eventually turned dark brown (Fig. S2C). The TEM image of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite was investigated in Fig. 1B. It was interesting that the square-like bulks of these nanocomposites were observed. The DLS of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite (Fig. S3) demonstrated an average diameter of 168 nm. To further investigate the morphology of as-prepared nanomaterials, SEM was carried out in Fig. 1C. The tridimensional structure of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite was clearly displayed and the thickness of these square-plate-like nanocomposite were measured between 60–70 nm. Fig. 1D showed the re-coloured SEM image of an individual bulk, in which plenty of small particles were anchored on the surface of the host material. Taking consideration of the match in size, these small particles were identified as GQDs demonstrating the successful integration of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite.

The elemental composition analysis of obtained nanocomposite was studied by XPS technique. Fig. S4 Fig. S4 recorded the full scan spectrum of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite indicating this material was composed of elements Mn 2p, O 1s, N 1s and C 1s. Element Mn and partial O were from MnO2; C and partial O were from GQDs and N came from PAH. To further examine the combination form, partial spectra of these elements were tested in Fig. 2 . Two component peaks at 653.9 and 642.4 eV were found in Mn 2p partial spectrum (Fig. 2A) and they can be identified to Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2, respectively. Fig. 2B displayed the O 1s spectrum and three peaks at 532.8, 531.4 and 529.8 eV stood for the combination form of H–O–H/C–O–H, Mn–O–H and Mn–O–Mn, respectively. The partial spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2C) can be divided into three peaks at 288.5, 286.0 and 284.8 eV, which stood for OC–OH, C–N and C–C, respectively. Fig. 2D showed the partial spectrum of N 1s, in which two peaks at 400.9 and 399.5 eV were obtained representing N–(C)3 and C–N–C, respectively. Overall, the above data proved the successful formation of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite to GSH
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrated the synthesis of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite and the GSH stimulated fluorescent turn-off-on process. Generally, GQDs and PAH were linked together firstly and formed a square-plate-like MnO2 nano-bulk after addition of KMnO4 with GQDs wrapped in. It was designed and anticipated that the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was quenched by MnO2 and subsequently restored by GSH due to the degradation of MnO2. To verify this assumption, the spectra study was carried out. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs and GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite and corresponding photos were displayed in Fig. 3 A. An obvious absorption around 200 nm was observed in GQDs and it still existed after integration with MnO2. While resultant MnO2 showed a wide absorption band ranging from 250 to 600 nm, indicating the favorable optical absorption property of MnO2. Fig. 3B recorded the fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs, GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite and GQDs-MnO2+GSH, respectively. GQDs showed a maximal fluorescence emission peak at 460 nm. Consistent with the theoretical assumption, fluorescent intensity was significantly quenched after integration with MnO2. But after the introduction of GSH, the fluorescence was recovered to a certain degree. Insets of Fig. 3B showed the obvious turn-off-on process of fluorescence under UV illumination.

In order to research the quenching mechanism, Fig. 3C compared the fluorescence emission of GQDs with UV–Vis absorption of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite. A wide range of overlap was indicated between these spectra (shade in Fig. 3C). Further, fluorescence lifetimes of GQDs before and after combination with MnO2 and that after reacting with GSH were tested in Fig. 3D and Table S1. The fluorescence lifetime of GQDs decreased from 2.03 ns to 1.67 ns after the formation of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite. As well know, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) took place once three conditions were met: (i) short distance (<10 nm) between donor and accepter; (ii) effective spectral overlap; (iii) decrease of fluorescence lifetime [36]. It was fairly obvious that GQDs wrapped in the MnO2 nanomaterial made the distance between them close enough. Coupling with the above results on the spectral overlap and fluorescence lifetime, the quenching mechanism was summarized to be FRET between GQDs and MnO2 nanomaterial. The energy transfer efficiency was demonstrated to be 17.73% referring to a reported calculation method [37]. Moreover, taking consideration of the spectral overlap between wide absorption of MnO2 (250–600 nm) and excitation (360 nm) and emission (460 nm) wavelength of GQDs, inner filter effect (IFE) was also contributed to the fluorescence quenching.

Further, GSH stimulated degradation of MnO2 was studied in Fig. S5 through UV–Vis absorption spectra. The wide absorption band of MnO2 disappeared after reaction with GSH meanwhile the inherent brown colour of MnO2 also faded (inset photos in Fig. S5), proving that GSH induced the decomposition of MnO2. Further, considering the dissociation performance of MnO2 by reacting with ascorbic acid (AA), Fig. S6 recorded the UV–Vis spectra of GQDs-MnO2 with AA and demonstrated the decomposition of MnO2, which further proved the sensing mechanism of GSH. Thus, the fluorescence recovery of GQDs triggered by GSH was convinced due to the decomposition of the quencher.

The above research proved that GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite possessed a GSH stimulated fluorescence response property and can be utilized as a fluorescent nanosensor for GSH sensing. Fig. 4 A showed the absorption intensity of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite at different time intervals after addition of GSH, in which absorption at 350 nm decreased gradually and remained unchanged after 30 min. Simultaneously, the fluorescence intensity variation of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite was recorded in Fig. 4B and the fluorescence began to flatten after 30 min was also concluded. The reaction between MnO2 and GSH was demonstrated to be completed after 30 min and therefore the time for GSH sensing was selected. Furthermore, the influences of HAc-NaAc buffers with different pH on GSH sensing were investigated in Fig. 4C and D. In general, GSH displayed good fluorescence recoveries under all the acidic and basic conditions owing to its good redox activity with MnO2. In detail, it was indicated that the fluorescence recovery efficiency (F/F0) decreased slightly along with the improvement of pH. The higher oxidation susceptibility of MnO2 under acidic conditions should take responsibility for this phenomenon. Finally, pH of 3 was chosen as the optimal condition because of its high recovery efficiency and almost the same under pH of 2.

Based on the above optimization of GSH response, the calibration curve of GSH detection was fitted from the fluorescence restoration efficiencies (F/F0) and GSH concentration (Fig. 5 A). It showed a linear relationship in the range of 0.07–70 μM and fitted the equation F/F0 = 1.1172 + 0.02264C (R2 = 0.9980), where C is the GSH concentration (μM), F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite in the absence and presence of GSH. The limit of detection was as low as 48 nM (3σ). To verify the practical application of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite for GSH detection, the selectivity of GSH determination against some amino acids and possible coexisting substances with equal concentration was carried out in Fig. 5B. The results indicated that no interferes can be observed in the presence of most of the amino acids and other coexisting substances. But Cys and Hcy caused, to some extent, fluorescence recovery because thiols existed in these molecules and it could also react with MnO2 and therefore recovered the fluorescence of GQDs. The same phenomenon was reported in other works about GSH sensing. It was worth noting that the concentrations of Cys and Hcy were much smaller than glutathione in biological tissues or cells [38,39]. Therefore, the interference of such low concentrations of Cys and Hcy was negligible on GSH determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GSH determination in sample and cancer cell discrimination
ce:section.ce:para: Taking advantage of the satisfactory sensitivity and selectivity for GSH sensing, GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite were utilized to the GSH detection in real sample. It was reported that the GSH contents in healthy human sera remained in the range of 400–1200 μM [40,41]. Combing with the diluted ratio, the data in Table 1 showed a satisfactory result that was consistent with previously reported works. Further, recoveries were calculated from three certain concentrations of GSH solutions to investigate the accuracy of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite for GSH sensing. The results showed that recoveries were in the range of 102.67%–106.84%, making the GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite based fluorescent turn-on method reliable.

Furthermore, GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite was utilized to the cancer cell recognition because GSH was overexpressed in cancer cells. The contents of GSH in cancer cells varied with the different types of cells over time at the level of several mM [42,43], and they were much higher than that in corresponding normal cells [44,45]. Therefore, the distinct difference on GSH content in cancerous cells against normal cells endowed GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite the ability to identify cancer cells. Cell viability in Fig. S7 Fig. S7 proved GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite showed little cytotoxicity. Fig. 6 A investigated the absorption intensity of MnO2 in buffers which simulated the cancer cell microenvironment. The decomposition rate of MnO2 showed a distinct discrepancy in the buffer similar to cancer cells microenvironment (PBS 5.0 + GSH) compared to other buffers. As a consequence, the fluorescence of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite recovered the fastest in PBS buffer with pH 5.0 + GSH accordingly (Fig. 6B). It was conceivable that an obvious fluorescence turn-on of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite in cancer cells can be achieved by higher content of GSH. For cell imaging, the confocal laser scanning microscope images of cells incubated with GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite were examined in Fig. 5C and D. Compared to the dim fluorescence in L02 cells (Fig. 6C), fluorescence was captured obviously in SMMC-7721 cells (Fig. 6D). Therefore, GSH-responsive GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite successfully differentiated cancer cells.

In summary, graphene quantum dots wrapped square-plate-like MnO2 nanocomposite was synthesized from KMnO4 under the ultrasonic condition with PAH being involved. It was found that the fluorescence of GQDs quenched in the presence of MnO2 through FRET and IFE. Further, GQDs@MnO2 nanocomposite showed a GSH stimulated fluorescent turn-on response and thus achieved GSH sensing. This nanosensor was constructed directly in GQDs through a in situ reaction to form a MnO2 based square-plate like nanocomposite, avoiding harsh reaction conditions or subsequent modification. The facile synthesis and novel morphology of nanocomposite would enrich the MnO2 nanocomposite-based applications in nanosensors.

Qi Wang: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Lingfang Li: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Xiaodong Wang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Chuan Dong: Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Shaomin Shuang: Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087), the Applied Basic Research Programs of Science and Technology Department of Shanxi Province (No. 201901D211454), the Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (STIP, No.2019L0918) and Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2017-Key1) for financial support.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxidized redox state of glutathione in the endoplasmic-reticulum
Science
